# Questions in Livestream 241

Live Stream #241: Decking the Halls with Poison Ivy

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=514" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:34</a> | Did grain magazine designs change over time enough to tell when they were built? | He said the dating usually comes from pottery and surrounding context rather than silo design alone. | He said silo form is not the normal dating basis because multiple magazine forms were used across periods. The stronger evidence is ceramic typology, associated finds such as dockets, and the architectural context; for example, magazines attached to the Temple of Atum belong with that Dynasty 19 precinct, while some smaller village silos can be earlier 18th Dynasty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=828" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:48</a> | Is rebaptism wrong, or can someone be baptized more than once? | He argued that baptism can be repeated if a later commitment to Christ is the genuine one. | He rejected the view that rebaptism is inherently sinful because he does not treat baptism as a repeated phenomenological bestowal of grace. He described baptism as a public promise to follow Christ, compared repeating it to renewing marriage vows, and said the "one baptism" language refers to Christ's saving work rather than forbidding a later public declaration. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=1207" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:07</a> | Which is more dangerous, the Jordan River or the Amazon River? | He said the Jordan is more dangerous, and added that the Nile is more dangerous than the Amazon as well. | He said the Jordan kills people yearly, whereas many people swim in parts of the Amazon without trouble. He also warned that the Nile is more dangerous than the Amazon because swimming in it can make people very sick, and added the Tigris to the list of rivers he would avoid. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=1261" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:01</a> | Does the Nile have hippos too? | He said north of the cataracts there are no hostile animals like hippos or crocodiles, though crocodiles are found south of the first cataract in Lake Aswan. | He distinguished sections of the Nile by the cataracts. In his answer, the stretch north of the first cataract lacks the dangerous animals people usually worry about, while crocodiles are present south of the first cataract in Lake Aswan, which is why he would not swim there. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=1328" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">22:08</a> | Did Christians ever get baptized in the Nile? | Yes. He said some Egyptian Christians did, though he would not recommend it today, especially for outsiders. | He said Nile baptisms have happened historically among early and Egyptian Christians. His caveat was practical rather than historical: he repeatedly said he would not recommend Westerners doing it today, given the earlier health and animal-safety concerns. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=1364" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">22:44</a> | Are bull sharks a danger in rivers like the Jordan or Nile? | He said bull sharks are not found in the Jordan or Nile, but can be encountered in rivers in places such as Australia or the eastern U.S. seaboard. | He described bull sharks as opportunistic and adaptable to both fresh and salt water, but said that does not make them a Jordan or Nile problem. The river danger is more relevant in places such as Australia or parts of the eastern United States where bull sharks can actually be encountered inland. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=1433" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:53</a> | Is it a good idea to collaborate with channels like Philosopher Garb or Testify Apologetics? | He said collaborations can be worthwhile, but he normally waits to be approached rather than chasing them himself. | He said he is often open to collaborations and sometimes accepts them, depending on timing and workload. His normal practice, however, is not to reach out and solicit collaborations; he waits for others to approach him and decides case by case. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=1651" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:31</a> | Why is the Christmas tree so itchy? | He blamed pine needles and sap, not a poison-ivy-style allergic reaction. | He said Christmas-tree irritation is usually mechanical and mild: people get poked by pine needles, and pine sap can be a mild irritant. He did not frame it as the same kind of allergic response caused by poison ivy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=1692" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:12</a> | Does poison ivy contaminate tea made with it? | Yes. He said the irritating oil survives heat, so using it in tea would still be dangerous. | He said the oil that causes poison ivy's rash does not break down with heat, so boiling it as tea would leave the drink toxic. He extended the warning to burning poison ivy: fire can vaporize the oil and spread it through smoke, so he said not to make tea from it or burn it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=1796" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">29:56</a> | Can goats or deer eat poison ivy? | He was unsure about goats, but said deer can eat poison ivy without trouble. | He did not claim knowledge about goats, but he was clear that deer can eat poison ivy without apparent trouble. He treated that as a reason deer might clear it, while joking that the tradeoff is having deer all over the lawn. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=1825" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:25</a> | Can poison ivy be composted? | No. He said composting poison ivy can spread it around and should be avoided. | He strongly warned against composting poison ivy because it can spread the plant rather than neutralize it. His practical advice in the surrounding answer was to avoid composting, burning, or otherwise reusing it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=1853" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:53</a> | If a deer eats poison ivy, does that poison the deer meat? | No. He said the deer digests it without passing the irritant into the meat. | He said meat from a deer that ate poison ivy is not contaminated by the plant. In his explanation, the deer can digest poison ivy, so the irritant does not carry through into the meat someone would eat. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=1921" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:01</a> | What evidence places the Semitic departure from Avaris in Ramesses II's reign rather than later centuries? | He appealed to the household goods, funerary context, and broader Dynasty 19 dating at the site. | He said the evidence comes from the overlap between household goods and funerary remains at Avaris, both dated through ceramic typology and related artifacts. Those lines of evidence end or begin around mid-Dynasty 19, and because Ramesses II's 66-year reign occupies such a large portion of that dynasty, he treated a mid-Dynasty 19 departure as effectively within Ramesses II's reign. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=2224" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:04</a> | If `men` is an earlier form of `ma`, when is the earliest `ma` found, why does the Torah not use `men`, and does that support an ancient manna tradition? | He rejected the premise that `men` is an older form of `ma` and said the proposed linguistic chain is too speculative. | He said the claim that `men` or `man` is an earlier form of the interrogative "what" is an unsupported assumption tied to explanations of manna. He checked the Pentateuchal manna passages and said they do not give a scribal gloss proving that derivation, so the argument for an ancient manna tradition based on that linguistic chain is putting too much weight on supposition. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=2547" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">42:27</a> | What translation of the Deuterocanon do you use? | He said he usually uses the New American Bible for the Apocrypha and Deuterocanon. | He identified the Deuterocanon with the Apocrypha for the question's purpose and said his normal English translation there is the New American Bible. He called it a good and artful translation, while clarifying that he does not use the New American Bible as his main translation elsewhere. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=2641" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">44:01</a> | Are vampires a kind of spirit critter? | No. He treated vampires as embodied undead rather than spirit beings. | He defined "spirit critter" narrowly as a being created ontologically as a spirit being without a body, and on that definition vampires do not qualify. He described vampires instead as undead cadavers that retain planning, premeditation, and moral reasoning, contrasting them with some werewolf traditions that involve union with a spirit being. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=2863" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:43</a> | Was Pazuzu a vampire? | He said he does not know Mesopotamian mythology well enough to answer that confidently. | He declined to answer beyond saying he did not know Mesopotamian mythology well enough for confidence on Pazuzu. The answer is intentionally limited rather than speculative. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=2906" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:26</a> | What did the primordial gods look like, and were they embodied? | He said they were not embodied, though Egyptian art later portrays them symbolically. | He said the primordial gods were not embodied as far as he knows, but Egyptian sources sometimes illustrate them as human-like figures submerged in the primordial waters. He treated those portrayals as symbolic depictions of spirit entities associated with creation and chaos, not as evidence that they had ordinary bodies. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=3052" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:52</a> | How do you manage the high throughput of your projects? | He said planning, quotas, and sustained focus are what let him keep producing. | He attributed his output to planning, daily quotas, limiting distractions such as social media, and simply continuing to write when doing first drafts. He especially emphasized quotas for projects that are hard or less enjoyable, describing the method as sustained focus rather than special magic. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=3170" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:50</a> | Are most names in Genesis placeholders because Hebrew was not the first language? | Not most. He said some names function like place names or ethnographic markers, but many cases remain uncertain. | He said some Genesis names, especially in Genesis 10, are better understood as toponyms or ethnographic labels rather than personal names, with Mizraim as an example. But he distinguished that from genealogical names in Genesis 5 and 11, where some may preserve real names or close forms, and concluded that the evidence is too limited to classify most names as placeholders. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=3362" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">56:02</a> | What languages are worth focusing on when translating Christian content? | He especially recommended languages such as Portuguese, Korean, Arabic, and Farsi because of their reach and mission value. | He recommended targeting languages where English is not already common as a second language. Portuguese and Korean were attractive because of large audiences and potential traction for Christian content, while Arabic and Farsi were useful for reaching harder-to-reach contexts; he did not prioritize major European languages like French or German for this purpose. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=3726" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:06</a> | What are some tips for lay people who want to examine the original Greek and Hebrew, and what traps should they avoid? | He said the best path is learning the languages themselves and avoiding shallow word-study shortcuts. | He said the real solution is to learn the languages, because each has its own trap. Hebrew is front-loaded: the alphabet and Masoretic points are the early obstacle, after which the language gets easier. Greek is the opposite: it feels manageable at first but becomes difficult in the second and third years because of nuance, especially categories such as the genitive and participles. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=3917" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:17</a> | Is learning classical Attic Greek sufficient? | He said it depends on the goal, but Attic takes longer than Koine and can require several years of study. | He said sufficiency depends on what someone wants to do, but Attic Greek is not a shortcut for biblical work. In his experience it takes longer than Koine, can require three to six years, and remains full of traps even for students who have already invested heavily in it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=3997" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:06:37</a> | If Greek takes years to learn, why not train AI to translate it? | He said AI cannot reliably handle the nuance needed for Greek translation and can only do crude surface translation. | He argued that the central challenge in Greek is nuance, not simply converting words across languages. AI can produce crude surface translation, but he said it cannot reliably make the contextual judgments needed for Greek exegesis, where the same form can have many possible meanings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=4144" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:09:04</a> | Is Greek hard to learn compared with English? | He said Greek is easier than German, harder than Hebrew, easier than Akkadian, and difficult mainly because of nuance rather than exceptions. | He contrasted English difficulty, which comes largely from exceptions, with Greek difficulty, which comes from contextual nuance. Greek forms can look simple, but categories such as the genitive may have many possible meanings, and choosing the wrong nuance can produce a clearly wrong translation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=4326" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:06</a> | Does classical Greek have slang? | He was unsure about classical Greek, but said Koine Greek certainly has regional slang. | He said he is not a classical Greek scholar and therefore would not state confidently whether classical Greek has slang. He was confident that Koine Greek has regional slang, including differences between Koine used in places such as Mesopotamia and Egypt. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=4377" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:57</a> | Is the common distinction between Greek words for love a false nuance? | Yes. He said the sharp eros, phileo, and agape distinction is modern and has been rejected by Greek scholars for a long time. | He said the familiar sermon distinction between `eros`, `phileo`, and `agape` is a modern false nuance dating to roughly the 19th century. In his view, Greek scholars and commentaries rejected the sharp distinction long ago, yet pastors continue using it as if it proves knowledge of Greek. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=4668" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:17:48</a> | Does God want people to make art such as books, comics, and other genres? | Yes. He said human creativity reflects God's own creativity and can be used faithfully. | He argued that because God is creative and made humans creative, making art can emulate one of God's traits. He connected art to cultural engagement and apologetic witness, saying stories, comics, games, music, and other forms can reach people at the level of heart and imagination as well as intellect. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=4855" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:55</a> | What themes do you explore in your Christian science-fiction novels without making them too preachy? | He said the themes are worked into the world and characters so the story carries them naturally rather than turning into a sermon. | He gave examples from his books: personal redemption takes tenacity, children become like their parents, prayer matters, and humility brings strength. He said the way to avoid preachiness is not to preach inside the story but to show universal truth, depict people living their faith, and present the world through a Christian lens. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=5321" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:28:41</a> | Do those themes intersect with the scumbag youth pastor character? | Not directly. He said that figure functions more as setup than as the central thematic vehicle. | He said the character is not where those themes mainly intersect. Instead, the character sets up the situation and provides a dark contrast against which the other characters' light and growth can be seen. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=5395" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:55</a> | Would God call someone to make works of art? | Yes. He compared that calling to other vocations and said art can be a real calling from God. | He said God can call people to be artists just as he can call people into other ordinary vocations. He cited Christian art history and Albrecht Durer as an example of a devout artist whose work and self-portraits reflected spiritual maturity and Christian conviction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=5604" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:24</a> | What inspired the premise of your series? | He said it grew out of his long-running interest in spirit beings and the unseen world. | He described the premise as coming from the biblical and cultural association of stars, angels, and the heavenly hosts. The fiction asks what happens if humanity goes to the stars and does not find angels there, while exiled androids begin filling that perceived role in space. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=5749" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:35:49</a> | Was the theme of All the Stars as Angels inspired by C.S. Lewis? | No. He said he was not a big fan of Lewis and instead drew the idea from biblical language about stars, hosts, and angels. | He said he was not drawing from C.S. Lewis, whose writing he personally found hard to get into. He traced the inspiration instead to Genesis language about the stars, the heavens and earth and their hosts, and the idea that the heavenly armies are associated with the angelic armies of God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=6250" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:44:10</a> | Are you done with the scumbag youth pastor character? | Yes. He said that character is finished and will not return. | He said the character is done because he was hanged in book two and is not coming back. He then shifted to noting that another character, Peter Chin, still has something planned later, but not that the youth pastor will return. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=6355" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:55</a> | What was casting lots like, and did it change over time? | He described lots as simple marked objects, often stick-like, and said the details varied across periods and cultures. | He said lots could be stick-like objects with marked and unmarked sides, functioning almost like two-sided dice, and that matching throws could determine an outcome. By New Testament times, "casting lots" could also cover dice-like gambling devices, such as the Roman dice used to decide who received Jesus' garment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=6843" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:54:03</a> | If the Eastern and Western churches ever reunited, where would that leave Protestants? | He thought Protestants would be in much the same position and doubted such a reunion would happen anyway. | He doubted reunion between East and West because of unresolved issues such as papal authority, the filioque, Marian doctrines, and other Catholic claims. Even if it happened, he said Protestants would not be materially repositioned because the central issues that separate them from Rome would remain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=7172" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:59:32</a> | Could schisms be part of God's plan? | He said yes, in the sense that God can work through church controversies and divisions to make the right course clearer. | He said God can work through schisms and controversies because the Holy Spirit has acted through the church's arguments to clarify the right course. His point was not that division is good in itself, but that God knew people would argue and could still guide the church through those conflicts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=7332" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:02:12</a> | What do you think of the Protestant idea that all sin is ultimately rooted in idolatry? | He said there is something to that, though he did not treat it as a complete explanation of every sin. | He said the Protestant claim works best using the New Testament sense of idolatry, where anything placed above God becomes idolatrous. On that definition, sins such as pride can be understood as loving or serving something above God, but he framed this as a valid biblical principle rather than a full technical definition of every sin. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=7527" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:05:27</a> | What is the definition of idolatry? | He distinguished Old Testament idolatry as trapping and extorting a god in an idol from the New Testament sense of putting anything above God. | He said Old Testament idolatry involves setting up an image, trapping a god in it, and extorting that god to get what the worshiper wants. He then related that to the New Testament idea that idolatry is putting anything above God, because the attempt to control a deity still elevates human wants over God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=7589" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:06:29</a> | Does a god escape if its idol breaks? | No. He said in that ancient framework breaking the idol harms or mars the god rather than freeing it; escape would come through ritual defilement of the sacred space. | He said a broken idol does not free the god but damages or mars it in that ancient framework. The way to make a god leave is to defile the ritual space so the sacred environment no longer contrasts with the profane outside, removing the conditions that keep the god in the idol. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=8012" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:13:32</a> | Could Avaris have begun as a cemetery associated with the plague of the firstborn? | He said it is possible and even plausible to him, but not something archaeology can prove. | He said Avaris appears to move from continuous occupation to cemetery use in mid-Dynasty 19, which makes a sudden catastrophic burial scenario plausible to him. But the cemetery was churned up by later agricultural leveling, so the archaeological evidence cannot prove that it began with plague-related mass burials. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=8266" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:17:46</a> | What are the similarities and differences between Elohim and Kami? | He said, in the small-g plural sense, both function as broad categories for supernatural beings with similar spheres or responsibilities. | He clarified that he was speaking of `elohim` as a numeric plural, not the majestic plural. In that small-g sense, he said Elohim and Kami are both broad labels for supernatural beings with spheres of influence, responsibilities, and mixed moral character, much like other cultures' categories for lesser divine beings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=8495" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:21:35</a> | Were all spirit critters eventually categorized simply as angels or demons, or is there a middle ground? | He said there is a middle ground and that not all spirit beings fit those two labels. | He said some spirit beings are neither angels nor demons, so the two categories do not exhaust the whole field. He immediately connected that answer to the next question about catch-bucket terminology, where broader labels can be used even when the technical categories are narrower. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=8530" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:22:10</a> | If demon is not a catchall term for evil spirits, why does the Bible always use demons as evil? | He said the Bible can use demons as a catch-bucket term for evil spirits even though, technically, not every evil spirit is a demon. | He compared "demon" to biblical catch-bucket labels such as Philistines, where several related groups can be grouped under one convenient term. Technically, he defined demons and angels as job descriptions: demons are evil beings sent to do tasks, and angels are messengers; other good or evil spirit beings may be grouped under those labels without fitting the job description strictly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=8942" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:29:02</a> | Is Kedorlaomer perhaps the same as an attested ancient ruler candidate? | He said maybe, but only as a tentative possibility among several candidates. | He said the proposed ruler is one possible candidate, but not the only one. There are at least three rulers from the relevant general period with names and backgrounds close enough to Kedorlaomer that he would not identify one with certainty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=9039" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:30:39</a> | Do modern angel encounters differ from biblical ones? | Yes. He said modern reports usually lack the qualitative force and clarity of biblical encounters. | He said modern reports, when credible, tend to resemble New Testament-style encounters in which angels take human-like forms and deliver a message or intervene without overwhelming the person. They differ from Old Testament prophetic throne-room visions, where beings are seen without human-like forms and the experience is qualitatively stranger and more intense. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=9294" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:34:54</a> | Could some historical or UFO encounters really be misunderstood angelic or spiritual manifestations? | Possibly. He allowed that some reports could be misunderstood spiritual encounters. | He discussed Clifford Wilson's argument that some UFO phenomena might be misunderstood demonic or spiritual manifestations and said that idea is not new. He was cautious, though, because many UFO reports have no spiritual message, so the theory might apply to some encounters without explaining all of them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=9752" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:42:32</a> | What did people believe in before UFOs? | He said people commonly reported fairies before the atomic age shifted the popular framework toward aliens. | He said earlier anomalous-encounter reports were commonly framed as fairies, with fairy sightings, rings, tunnels, and otherworldly stories before World War II. After the atomic age, he said the interpretive framework quickly shifted from fairies to aliens. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=9996" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:46:36</a> | Are alien reports more human propaganda from World War II or the Cold War, and have aliens visited Earth? | He said many alien views come from Cold War propaganda, he does not think aliens have visited Earth, but he thinks other life elsewhere is statistically plausible. | He connected many modern alien ideas to Cold War propaganda and secrecy after the atomic bomb, when governments had reasons to keep rivals off balance. He denied that aliens have visited Earth, while saying the vastness of galaxies and stars makes extraterrestrial civilizations possible in principle. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=10368" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:52:48</a> | What do you think of pro-Mosaic-law theology? | He was strongly negative and called it a Judaizing error that fails to reckon with fulfillment in Christ. | He said he has a very low view of Pro-Mosaic-law or Hebrew Roots approaches because the law has been fulfilled in Christ and salvation is by grace through faith. He treated attempts to revive Mosaic-law obligation as modern Judaizing rather than as sound Christian theology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=10423" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:53:43</a> | Would encountering life on another planet alter the gospel? | No. He said it would raise theological questions about those beings' fallen or unfallen status, but it would not change the gospel. | He said alien life would not affect the gospel itself, but it would raise questions about those beings' spiritual status. If unfallen, they might need no salvation; if fallen, Christians would have to ask what provision God made for them, but those questions would not alter the gospel revealed for humanity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=10602" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:56:42</a> | Does the work of John Walton and others on Torah undermine Pro-Nomian theology? | He said New Testament scholarship, more than Walton's work, is what undermines that modern form of Judaizing. | He said Walton does not really address Pro-Nomian theology directly enough to be the main issue. The more decisive undermining comes from New Testament scholarship and its teaching about the law, fulfillment, grace, and the modern Judaizing character of Pro-Nomian claims. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=10665" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:57:45</a> | How do we know Isaiah 44:6 is speaking of two Yahwehs rather than mere poetic language, and does the same issue arise elsewhere in Isaiah? | He argued the surrounding grammar and speaker shifts support the two-Yahweh reading. | He contrasted Isaiah 54:5, where "maker" and "redeemer" could be poetic or ambiguous, with Isaiah 44:6, where the possessive "his redeemer" has the Lord, king of Israel, as its antecedent. For him, that grammar means one Yahweh figure possesses or is related to another figure identified as Yahweh of hosts, making the two-Yahweh reading grammatically stronger. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=11351" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:09:11</a> | Why have Jews viewed Christians negatively for millennia? | He pointed to a history of persecutions and hostility in both directions, especially once Christians gained political power. | He said the hostility has historical reasons, including persecutions on both sides in the early period. Once Christians gained political power, he said persecution of Jews continued in ways that became much more consequential, with the Spanish Inquisition as one example of abuses tied to politics and asset seizure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=11484" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:11:24</a> | Does God believe in term limits or a standard retirement age for priests? | He answered that the biblical pattern is a retirement age, not term limits. | He answered briefly that the biblical model is not term limits but a standard retirement age for priests and Levites. He did not develop the point further before the discussion moved back to historical persecution issues. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=11620" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:13:40</a> | Does Surah 70:40 support a Quranic two-powers theology? | He was cautious and said the plural pronoun could be a royal or corporate "we," so that passage alone did not clearly prove two powers. | He said the plural "we" in the quoted passage could be a majestic, royal, or corporate plural, so it is too ambiguous by itself to establish a two-powers theology. He contrasted that with biblical cases where possessive pronouns provide stronger grammatical evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=11868" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:17:48</a> | Did the magnificent plural exist in ancient Hebrew, and could Genesis 1:26 be about the Trinity? | He said the magnificent plural did exist in ancient Hebrew, and that this does not rule out Trinitarian significance. | He said the majestic or magnificent plural did exist in ancient Hebrew, giving the Tower of Babel language as an example. At the same time, he said the existence of the plural category does not negate Trinitarian readings; the grammar and theological interpretation can both be considered. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=11978" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:19:38</a> | Is there significance to Moses being hidden for three months? | Not much beyond ordinary infancy. He said the period reflects the stage before concealment became impossible. | He said the three months are probably practical rather than symbolic: as an infant grows, crying and noise make concealment impossible, especially in closely packed houses. He initially wondered about walking age, then settled on noise as the relevant factor. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=12169" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:22:49</a> | Are there recommended resources on the historical development of skepticism about the Exodus? | He did not know a single resource on that development, but pointed to biblical minimalist scholars such as Lemche, Davies, Thompson, and to a lesser extent Dever. | He said Exodus skepticism shifted especially with the minimalist movement of the 1990s after earlier commentators often took the Exodus more for granted. He did not know a dedicated history of that development, but suggested looking at minimalist scholars such as Lemche, Davies, Thompson, and to a lesser extent Dever. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=12249" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:24:09</a> | How should someone help the poor or give alms? | He recommended giving through local vetted charities that can use purchasing power and screen real needs better than an individual can. | He said established local charities are usually the best route, especially in the West, because they can leverage purchasing power, vet recipients, and handle fairness better than an individual donor. He specifically mentioned food banks and other vetted local organizations as examples. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=12333" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:25:33</a> | How can someone verify that a charity is legitimate? | He recommended using outside charity evaluators and vetting organizations that review other charities. | He said to use accountability groups and charity trackers that review charities' legitimacy, management, finances, tax filings, and administration costs. He added that legitimate charities typically make financial information public and explain how donations are used. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=12402" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:26:42</a> | Should people avoid giving money directly because of scammers or drug and alcohol misuse? | He said that is a real problem in the West and again recommended established charities because they can vet needs and recipients. | He said direct giving is especially difficult in Western contexts because scams and substance misuse are real problems and individuals usually lack the resources to distinguish needs well. In his view, it is easier and wiser to vet one charity than to vet every person asking for help. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=12591" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:29:51</a> | If someone on the street might be an angel in disguise, should you give money directly? | He said the biblical theme does not require unwise giving; helping through a bona fide charity can still be a faithful way to serve. | He said the "angels in disguise" theme comes from a different social context and does not require irresponsible use of resources. Giving through a bona fide charity can still honor the biblical impulse to help, while reducing the risk that money, food, or gift certificates will be resold for harmful purposes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=13238" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:40:38</a> | How were the ancient poor different from today's poor? | He said ancient poverty was often a life-or-death condition marked by starvation, disability, and severe nutritional disease. | He described ancient poverty as being on the cusp of death, where a small caloric deficit could mean starvation within days. He contrasted that with modern Western poverty, noting that even poor people today may have more calories and diet variety than ancient elites, while ancient poor often suffered conditions such as vitamin-A-related blindness and lameness from malnutrition. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=13694" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:48:14</a> | What about Jonathan's son, Mephibosheth, whom David looked after? | He said Mephibosheth was different because his lameness came from an accident rather than the nutritional poverty he was discussing. | He said Mephibosheth's lameness was caused by an accident, possibly an injury, not by the nutritional deficits he had been discussing. That makes him a different case from the majority of ancient lame or blind poor, though he may still have been vulnerable or near destitute. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=14182" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:56:22</a> | Outside Genesis, where does God employ the majestic plural? | He said it is mostly in Genesis, can be recalled at least through the Tower of Babel, and may also appear in Isaiah or Ezekiel but would need more checking. | He said the majestic plural is mostly visible in Genesis and that he could recall it at least as late as the Tower of Babel. He thought Isaiah, and possibly Ezekiel, might contain further examples, but he explicitly said he would need to look into those more deeply. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=14277" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:57:57</a> | Does Carpenter's three-month Sinai reading connect to Moses' infancy and the three-day wilderness request? | He said he appreciated the perspective but thought the proposed pattern was a stretch and did not track well. | He understood the proposal as connecting Israel's three months to Sinai with Moses' three months in infancy and the three-day wilderness request. He said the sequence does not track well because Moses' rescue pattern and Israel's Red Sea-to-Sinai pattern are ordered differently, so he appreciated the idea but regarded it as stretched. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qN_xzWvDxI4?t=14453" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:00:53</a> | If a Bible-study discussion never seems to go anywhere, how can it become more productive? | He recommended a competent leader who can read, exegete, and apply the passage rather than simply collecting everyone's opinions. | He said unproductive Bible studies often read a passage and then circle the room asking for everyone's impressions, which leaves people with disconnected opinions rather than edification. A better study needs a leader who knows the material, handles a small passage, reads it, exegetes it, applies it, and shows participants things in the text they would not have seen alone. |
